overview.body: This is an ENHANCE week — you'll transform your ugly if/elif chains into elegant parallel lists! Remember all those repetitive destination menus and choice handlers? Lists make them disappear. You'll also add tracking lists to record your route and hazards, and update save/load to preserve them.

mentorQuote.quote: Here's a question I ask every developer: if you had to add 20 more cities to your game, how long would it take? If the answer is 'hours of copying and pasting,' your code isn't scalable. Lists fix that. Add a city? Add one line to a list. That's it. Five minutes of planning saves five hours of debugging.

objectives: display_destinations() — Loop through lists to show menu dynamically
Enhanced Save/Load — Preserve tracking lists with a separator marker
overview.keyConceptsText: Chapter 7: 7.2 (List Creation), 7.3 (for loops with lists), 7.4 (in operator), 7.5 (index, remove, copy)
overview.textbookReference: Starting Out with Python, 6th Edition by Tony Gaddis — Chapter 7
programRequirements: Create parallel lists for three origin cities (San Antonio, Houston, Austin)
San Antonio now has 4 destinations (Corpus Christi, Houston, Austin, Laredo)
Houston now has 3 destinations (Galveston, San Antonio, Dallas)
Laredo and Dallas are dead ends — no onward connections
Track visited cities and hazards encountered in lists
Save/load must preserve both tracking lists using a separator
overview.filenameNote: Submit TWO files this week: function_library.py — Your expanded module (now with file utility functions) and lab-3-2.py — Your game with parallel lists and tracking.
overview.additionalFilesNote: function_library.py is submitted alongside lab-3-2.py — it gets three new file utility functions. CodeGrade tests these functions in isolation.

rows.task: Build function to loop through and display menu
Display route and hazards from lists

timeGuide.strategy: Lists are powerful but easy to mess up. Test after each checkpoint! Make sure your menus display correctly before moving on. Verify indexing works with actual gameplay. Don't save until you're sure the tracking lists are being built correctly.

beforeYouBegin.intro: Here's how your finished program fits together. [NEW] = brand new code. [REPLACED] = old code swapped for something better.
beforeYouBegin.architecture:
# ---- CONSTANTS ----
SA_DESTINATIONS = ['Corpus Christi', 'Houston', 'Austin', 'Laredo']  [NEW]
SA_DISTANCES    = [150, 200, 80, 160]                               [NEW]
SA_NOTES        = ['BEACH!', 'Gateway East', ...]                    [NEW]
(same pattern for HOUSTON_ and AUSTIN_ lists)

# ---- FUNCTION LIBRARY (function_library.py) ----
All Lab 2 functions still here, plus:
save_list_to_file()    [NEW]
load_list_from_file()   [NEW]
file_exists()          [NEW]

# ---- HELPER FUNCTIONS ----
display_destinations(city)    [REPLACED old print blocks]
get_destination(city, choice) [REPLACED old if/elif chains]
get_redirect(city, avoid)     [NEW — handles detours]

# ---- TRACKING LISTS ----
visited_cities = ['San Antonio']   [NEW]
hazards_list = []                   [NEW]

# ---- MAIN MENU (same as 3.1) ----
(Load now restores tracking lists too)

GAME LOOP:
│
│── display_destinations(current_city)     [REPLACED]
│── Get validated choice
│── get_destination(current_city, choice)  [REPLACED]
│── Destination Gate (coin flip)
│
│   TRAVEL LOOP:
│   │── Roll D20, move miles
│   │── Check for hazard
│   │      If hazard: hazards_encountered.append(name)  [NEW]
│   │── Repeat until arrival
│
│── visited_cities.append(destination)     [NEW]
│── Update trip totals
│── Check outcome / Save & Quit option

# ---- AFTER THE LOOP ----
Enhanced trip report:
  Route: San Antonio -> Houston -> Galveston   [NEW]
  Hazards encountered list                        [NEW]
"Thanks for playing!"

checkpoints.title: Create Parallel Lists
checkpoints.subtitle: Set up the destination, distance, and notes lists for each origin city.

content.quote: This week you're replacing a LOT of ugly code with clean code. All those if/elif chains for destinations? Gone — replaced by lists. All those scattered print statements for menus? Gone — replaced by functions. The game loop itself barely changes. You're swapping out parts, not rebuilding the engine.

content.body: Parallel lists — three lists where the same index across all three refers to related data. SA_DESTINATIONS[0] is 'Corpus Christi', SA_DISTANCES[0] is 150, SA_NOTES[0] is 'BEACH!'.

Parallel lists are your bridge to more sophisticated data structures. While we'll learn better approaches (dictionaries, objects), understanding how to keep data synchronized across multiple lists is a fundamental skill. Many real-world systems still use this pattern, and debugging parallel list issues is a rite of passage for every programmer.

items: Copy your lab-3-1.py and rename it lab-3-2.py
Update the docstring: "Lab 3.2: Save Your Progress"
Near the top (after imports, before functions), add a comment section: # PARALLEL LISTS
Create the San Antonio lists (4 destinations now!)
Create the Houston lists (3 destinations)
Create the Austin lists (2 destinations)

references.description: You created a list with initial values using sales = [0] * NUM_DAYS. Here you're doing something similar but with actual values: ['Corpus Christi', 'Houston', ...]

content.body: Step 1: Add temporary test code at the bottom of your file to verify your lists:

content.code:
# TEMPORARY TEST CODE - Delete before submitting
print('SA destinations:', SA_DESTINATIONS)
print('SA distances:', SA_DISTANCES)
print('Index 1 test:', SA_DESTINATIONS[1], '-', SA_DISTANCES[1], 'miles')

content.body: Step 2: Run your file and verify the output:

content.output:
SA destinations: ['Corpus Christi', 'Houston', 'Austin', 'Laredo']
SA distances: [150, 200, 80, 160]
Index 1 test: Houston - 200 miles

content.body: Step 3: Verify these critical behaviors: Index alignment — Index 1 returns Houston (200 miles), not Corpus Christi. List lengths match — All three SA lists have 4 items. No typos — City names and distances are correct.
Step 4: Delete the test code, then move on to Checkpoint 2.

rows: Parallel lists MUST have same length — index 3 needs to exist in all three!
Data out of sync across lists
If index 1 is Houston in destinations, it must be 200 in distances
Constants should be SA_DESTINATIONS not sa_destinations

items: 9 constant lists defined at module level (3 cities — 3 lists each)
All lists use UPPERCASE_NAMES (they're constants)
Index 0 in all three SA lists refers to Corpus Christi data

checkpoints.title: Build display_destinations()
checkpoints.subtitle: Create a function that loops through your parallel lists to display the menu.

content.body: A function called display_destinations(city) that takes a city name, loops through the appropriate lists, prints a numbered menu, and returns the number of choices.

items: Define display_destinations(city) with one parameter
Use if/elif to select which lists to use based on city
Add an else case that prints "No destinations available" and returns 0
Print 'Where would you like to go?'
Loop with for i in range(len(destinations)):
Inside loop: Build a line with menu number, destination, distance, and note
Return len(destinations)

references.description: You used for index in range(len(sales)) to loop through indices. That's exactly what you need here — the index lets you access the same position across all three parallel lists!

rows: Caller can't set valid range for get_valid_int()
Messy output, harder to format consistently

content.body: Step 1: Add temporary test code at the bottom of your file:

content.code:
# TEMPORARY TEST CODE - Delete before submitting
print('Testing San Antonio:')
count = display_destinations('San Antonio')
print(f'Returned count: {count}')
print()
print('Testing Houston:')
count = display_destinations('Houston')
print(f'Returned count: {count}')

content.body: Step 2: Run your file and verify the output:

content.output:
Testing San Antonio:
  1. Corpus Christi - 150 miles (BEACH!)
  2. Houston - 200 miles (Gateway East)
  3. Austin - 80 miles (Keep it Weird)
  4. Laredo - 160 miles (Dead End)
Returned count: 4

Testing Houston:
  1. Galveston - 50 miles (BEACH!)
  2. San Antonio - 200 miles (Back West)
  3. Dallas - 240 miles (Wrong Way)
Returned count: 3

content.body: Step 3: Verify these critical behaviors: Numbers start at 1, not 0. San Antonio returns 4, Houston returns 3, Austin returns 2. Notes appear in parentheses after each item.
Step 4: Delete the test code, then move on to Checkpoint 3.

items: display_destinations(city) that prints a numbered menu
Returns the number of choices (4 for San Antonio, 3 for Houston, 2 for Austin)
Handles unknown cities gracefully (prints message, returns 0)

checkpoints.title: Build get_destination() and get_redirect()
checkpoints.subtitle: Replace your if/elif choice handlers with elegant list indexing.

content.body: Two functions: get_destination(city, choice) returns the destination name and distance using [choice - 1] indexing. get_redirect(city, avoid) picks a random different destination for detours.

items: For get_destination(city, choice): Define function with two parameters: city and choice
Calculate index: index = choice - 1
Use if/elif to determine which city's lists to access
Return a tuple: return SA_DESTINATIONS[index], SA_DISTANCES[index]
Add else case returning 'San Antonio', 100
For get_redirect(city, avoid): Define function with two parameters: city and avoid
Use if/elif to select city lists, then make copies: destinations = SA_DESTINATIONS.copy()
Check if avoid is in the list: if avoid in destinations:
Find its position: idx = destinations.index(avoid)
Remove from both copies: destinations.remove(avoid) and distances.pop(idx)
Pick random from remaining using random.randint(0, len(destinations) - 1)

references.description: You used the in operator to check if a product number existed in a list. Same pattern here: if avoid in destinations:
You used index() to find an item's position and remove() to delete it. For parallel lists, you also need pop(idx) to remove by position.

content.title: CRITICAL: Why .copy() is Essential
content.body: If you write destinations = SA_DESTINATIONS, both variables point to the same list. When you remove an item, you delete it from your original constant! Always use .copy() when you need to modify a list without affecting the original.
Step 1: Add temporary test code at the bottom of your file:

content.code:
# TEMPORARY TEST CODE - Delete before submitting
dest, dist = get_destination('San Antonio', 2)
print(f'Choice 2 from SA: {dest} - {dist} miles')

redirect = get_redirect('San Antonio', 'Houston')
print(f'Redirect (avoiding Houston): {redirect}')

# Verify .copy() worked - original should still have Houston
print('Original SA_DESTINATIONS:', SA_DESTINATIONS)

content.body: Step 2: Run your file and verify the output:

content.output:
Choice 2 from SA: Houston - 200 miles
Redirect (avoiding Houston): Corpus Christi   (or Austin or Laredo - random!)
Original SA_DESTINATIONS: ['Corpus Christi', 'Houston', 'Austin', 'Laredo']

content.body: Step 3: Verify these critical behaviors: Choice 2 returns index 1 (Houston), not index 2 (Austin). Redirect never returns Houston (the city we're avoiding). Original list unchanged — still has all 4 cities including Houston.
Step 4: Delete the test code, then move on to Checkpoint 4.

rows: User enters 1 for first item, but index 0 is first — subtract 1!
Use .copy() to create a copy before removing the avoided city
Forgetting to return BOTH destination and distance
Function returns a tuple — caller needs both values

content.quote: Dude, when this clicked for me, I literally said 'OHHHH' out loud. The user picks choice 2, you subtract 1 to get index 1, and boom — you've got Houston and 200 miles without a single if statement. Compare your code to last week's version. That's real progress. You're not the same coder you were seven days ago.

items: get_destination() returns (destination, distance) tuple using list indexing
get_redirect() returns a random different destination
Update your main game loop to call these functions instead of if/elif chains

checkpoints.title: Add Tracking Lists & Enhanced Save/Load
checkpoints.subtitle: Track your route and hazards in lists, then update save/load to preserve them.

content.body: Two tracking lists (visited_cities and hazards_list) that grow as you play. Updated save_game() and load_game() that preserve these lists using a ---HAZARDS--- separator.

items: Part A — Initialize Tracking Lists. In new game setup: visited_cities = ['San Antonio'] (start with home city). Also add: hazards_list = [] (empty until hazards happen). After arriving at destination: visited_cities.append(destination). When hazard occurs: hazards_list.append(hazard) (where hazard is the message string).

Part B — Update save_game(). Add two new parameters: visited_cities and hazards_list. After writing basic stats, loop through visited_cities and write each city. Write the separator: outfile.write('---HAZARDS---\n'). Loop through hazards_list and write each hazard.

Part C — Update load_game(). After reading basic stats, create empty visited_cities = []. Use a while loop to read lines until you hit '---HAZARDS---'. Append each city to visited_cities. Create empty hazards_list = []. Continue reading and appending until end of file. Return tuple now includes both lists.

Part D — Add File Functions to function_library.py. These three utility functions live in function_library.py, not in your main game file. Add them under a new # FILE FUNCTIONS comment block after your existing validation functions.

references.description: You built a list dynamically with name_list = [] then name_list.append(name). Same pattern here — start empty, append as events happen!
Writing a list to a file one item per line — exactly what save_list_to_file() does.
Reading a file back into a list — that's load_list_from_file(). The readline() while loop pattern is identical to your load_game() from Lab 3.1.

content.body: For function_library.py — save_list_to_file(filename, data_list): Define the function with two parameters: filename and data_list. Open the file for writing: with open(filename, 'w') as outfile:. Loop through data_list and write each item: outfile.write(str(item) + '\n'). No return value needed.

For function_library.py — load_list_from_file(filename): Define the function with one parameter: filename. Create an empty result list: result = []. Open the file inside a try block: with open(filename, 'r') as infile:. Use a readline() while loop — same pattern as your load_game(). Append each line stripped of \n to result. In the except FileNotFoundError block: leave result as empty list. Return result.

For function_library.py — file_exists(filename): Define the function with one parameter: filename. In a try block: attempt to open the file for reading. If it opens successfully: return True. In the except FileNotFoundError block: return False.

content.title: Why the ---HAZARDS--- marker?
content.body: The ---HAZARDS--- marker separates the variable-length visited_cities list from the variable-length hazards_list. Since both lists can be any length, you need a clear signal for where one ends and the other begins.
Step 1: Play a short game, save mid-way, then check your save file — open savegame.txt in a text editor and verify contents.
Step 2: Your save file should look like this:

content.output:
TestPlayer
Houston
280
3
2
1
San Antonio
Austin
Houston
---HAZARDS---
Speed trap!
Armadillo crossing!

content.body: Step 3: Quit and reload — verify these behaviors: Route restored — visited_cities has San Antonio, Austin, Houston. Hazards restored — hazards_list has the 2 hazard messages. No duplicates — Separator line itself is NOT in either list.
Step 4: Once save/load works correctly, move on to Checkpoint 5.

rows: The separator is a marker, not data — skip it during load
Newlines in data cause comparison failures and display bugs
Empty lists must exist before you can append to them

items: visited_cities grows each time you arrive somewhere
hazards_list grows each time a hazard occurs
Save/load preserves both lists using the separator marker
Three new functions added to function_library.py: save_list_to_file(), load_list_from_file(), file_exists()
Test: Save mid-game, reload, verify lists are restored correctly

checkpoints.title: Enhanced Trip Report
checkpoints.subtitle: Display your route and hazards using numbered lists in the final report.

content.body: An enhanced trip report that displays your complete route as a numbered list and all hazards encountered. This shows the player exactly where they traveled and what they faced!

items: After the game loop, print 'YOUR ROUTE:'
Loop through visited_cities with for i in range(len(visited_cities)):
Print each city with number: f' {i + 1}. {visited_cities[i]}'
Print 'HAZARDS ENCOUNTERED:'
Check if len(hazards_list) > 0: before looping
If no hazards, print ' None - Lucky traveler!'

content.output:
==================================================
TRIP REPORT
==================================================
Driver: Maria
Final City: Galveston

YOUR ROUTE:
  1. San Antonio
  2. Houston
  3. Galveston

HAZARDS ENCOUNTERED:
  1. Buc-ee's appears!
  2. Speed trap!

STATISTICS:
  Total Miles: 267
  Total Turns: 4
  Hazards: 1/2 escaped
==================================================

content.body: Step 1: Play a full game to a beach, then check the trip report.

Step 2: Verify the trip report shows: Numbered route — 1. San Antonio, 2. Houston, 3. Galveston. Numbered hazards — Each hazard message with a number. Statistics — Miles, turns, and hazard escape ratio.
Step 3: Test the "no hazards" case — play a quick game (San Antonio to Corpus Christi is fastest). If no hazards occurred, report should say "None - Lucky traveler!"
Step 4: Once your trip report displays correctly, you're ready to submit!

rows: Use i + 1 to start numbering at 1
Not checking if hazards list is empty
If no hazards, display "None" instead of empty section
Division by zero in escape ratio

items: Trip report shows numbered route from tracking list
Hazards section shows all encountered hazards (or "None")
Statistics section preserved from Lab 3.1

beforeYouSubmit: lab-3-2.py with parallel lists, tracking lists, and enhanced save/load
9 parallel lists defined as constants (UPPERCASE names)
Both files have proper header comments with your name, ID, and section
Save/load uses ---HAZARDS--- separator and restores both tracking lists
finalChecklist.scopeReminder: You may use all Chapter 2-7 concepts including lists with initial values, append(), len(), in operator, index(), remove(), pop(), copy(), for with range(len()). NOT YET: sort() (that's Lab 3.3!), list slicing, list comprehensions, readlines().

specific: All previous techniques (variables, if/else, loops, functions, file I/O)
Lists with initial values, append(), len(), in operator
permittedTechniques.notPermitted: sort() (Lab 3.3), list slicing, list comprehensions, readlines()

finalChecklist.academicIntegrity: This assignment must be your own work. You may reference the textbook and BookEx examples. You may NOT copy code from others, use AI, or share your code.
finalChecklist.saveYourCode: Keep BOTH files! In Lab 3.3, you'll add sort(), unique city counting, and a printable trip report file. Your function library will keep growing!

ifYouGetStuck: Re-read BookEx 7.2 and 7.5 — list creation, indexing, and modification patterns
Test your parallel lists first — make sure indexing works before building functions
Print intermediate values — show what's in your lists at key points
Submit to CodeGrade early — shows exactly which tests pass or fail
Come to Zoom or office hours — screen share and debug together
